<uploaded_files>
<file><file_path>/Users/jay/Library/CloudStorage/Dropbox/Misc/IP Addresses 2020.xlsx</file_path><file_uuid>d5fd87e3-e4af-49d0-bace-eeb9c2dc8611</file_uuid></file>
</uploaded_files>

Attached is the IP address the static addresses in my home network. This is for the containers and servers and I've been using this spreadsheet now for over 5 years, but actually I'd like to have a tool that is gives me a better visual view, allows me to look up IP addresses very quickly, what services are running on that IP address, what the server name is, what the container, etc. And is a quick and easy better way than using Excel spreadsheets. I think it's better using HTML React and a clean intuitive interface.

content.text: I'll start by reading your Excel file to understand the structure of your IP address data, then create a clean, interactive React-based tool for you.

input.command:
pip install openpyxl pandas --break-system-packages -q && python3 -c "
import pandas as pd
import json

# Read all sheets from the Excel file
xlsx = pd.ExcelFile('/sessions/keen-focused-galileo/mnt/uploads/IP Addresses 2020.xlsx')
print('Sheet names:', xlsx.sheet_names)
print()

for sheet in xlsx.sheet_names:
    print(f'=== Sheet: {sheet} ===')
    df = pd.read_excel(xlsx, sheet_name=sheet)
    print(f'Columns: {list(df.columns)}')
    print(f'Rows: {len(df)}')
    print()
    print(df.to_string())
    print()
    print()
"

I've created an interactive IP Address Manager for your home network. Here's what it offers:

**Key Features:**

- **Instant Search** — Type any IP, hostname, service name, or location to filter instantly
- **Smart Filtering** — Dropdown filters for Virtual/Physical type and location (Garage, Office, House, Proxmox hosts)
- **Two View Modes** — Cards view for visual browsing, Table view for quick scanning
- **Reserved IP Toggle** — Hide/show your reserved IP blocks (hidden by default to reduce clutter)
- **Stats Dashboard** — Quick overview showing 64 active, 47 virtual, 17 physical, 23 reserved addresses
- **Color-Coded Badges** — Purple for Virtual, Blue for Physical, unique colors per location
- **Service Icons** — Cameras, servers, Docker, DNS, storage, networking gear each get recognizable icons
- **Expandable Cards** — Click any card to see full details

**Your data includes:**
- 6 Proxmox hosts across Garage and Office
- 4 cameras (Reolink + Tapo)
- Multiple Unifi networking devices
- Media stack (Plex, Radarr, Sonarr, etc.)
- DNS infrastructure (3 Pihole instances)
- Ghost blogs, Docker hosts, TrueNAS storage, and more

[View your IP Address Manager](computer:///sessions/keen-focused-galileo/mnt/outputs/ip-address-manager.jsx)

    const parts = item.ip.split('.');
    return parseInt(parts[3]);
  }));

  const freeIPs = [];
  // Only check static range (171-254)
  for (let i = NETWORK_CONFIG.staticStart; i <= NETWORK_CONFIG.staticEnd; i++) {
    if (!usedIPs.has(i)) {
      freeIPs.push(`192.168.0.${i}`);
    }
  }
  return freeIPs;
};

This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

Analysis:
Let me chronologically analyze the conversation:

1. **Initial Request**: User uploaded an Excel spreadsheet "IP Addresses 2020.xlsx" containing their home network static IP addresses. They've been using this spreadsheet for 5 years but wanted a better visual tool - specifically HTML/React with a clean intuitive interface for:
   - Quick IP address lookup
   - View services running on each IP
   - See server names and container info
   - Better than Excel spreadsheets

2. **First Action**: I read the Excel file using pandas/openpyxl. Found it had 87 rows with columns: AssetName, Hostname, IP Address, Virtual/Physical, Location, Apps. The data ranged from 192.168.0.6 to 192.168.0.254 with many "Reserved" empty entries.

3. **Created Initial React Component**: Built `/sessions/keen-focused-galileo/mnt/outputs/ip-address-manager.jsx` with:
   - Search functionality
   - Filtering by type (Virtual/Physical) and location
   - Cards and Table views
   - Color-coded badges for locations and types
   - Service icons based on app/asset names
   - Stats dashboard
   - Toggle for showing reserved IPs

4. **User Feedback #1**: User asked to show how many free addresses and where they are so they can quickly see them.

5. **Added Free IPs Feature**: Added expandable panel showing free IPs, grouped into ranges, with click-to-copy functionality.

6. **User Feedback #2**: User clarified that Pihole runs DHCP from .1 to .170, with .6 and .50 being fixed IPs within that range. So the "free" calculation was wrong - addresses in .1-.170 aren't really free, they're DHCP pool.

7. **Updated Network Configuration**: Added NETWORK_CONFIG object with:
   - dhcpStart: 1, dhcpEnd: 170
   - staticStart: 171, staticEnd: 254
   - fixedInDHCP: [6, 50]
   - Updated UI to show DHCP/Fixed/Static badges
   - Free IPs now only calculated from static range

8. **User Feedback #3**: User uploaded new spreadsheet and noted green highlighted rows are free IPs.

9. **Parsed Excel Colors**: Used openpyxl to read cell fill colors. Found:
   - FF00B050 = Green (Free): .184, .206, .251, .253
   - FFFF0000 = Red: .189 (printer)
   - FFFFFF00 = Yellow: .224

10. **Updated with Green-Highlighted Free IPs**: Changed the 4 IPs to "Free" in data and updated NETWORK_CONFIG.freeIPs array. Added special styling for Free IPs - green cards, AVAILABLE badge, one-click copy.

11. **User Feedback #4 (Most Recent)**: User provided list of ALL free IPs:
    - 192.168.0.171-187 (17 IPs)
    - 192.168.0.194, 195, 206 (3 IPs)
    - Total: 20 free IPs

12. **Current Work**: Started updating the freeIPs array in NETWORK_CONFIG with all 20 IPs. Also started changing the ipData entries from "Reserved" to "Free" - completed one replacement (.171).

Key files:
- `/sessions/keen-focused-galileo/mnt/uploads/IP Addresses 2020.xlsx` - original data

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   User has been managing home network static IP addresses in an Excel spreadsheet for 5+ years. They wanted a better tool with:
   - Clean, intuitive React/HTML interface
   - Quick IP address lookup
   - View services running on each IP
   - See server names, container info, and locations
   - Visual display of free/available IPs for quick assignment
   - Understanding of network layout (DHCP pool vs static range)

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - React functional components with hooks (useState, useMemo)
   - Tailwind CSS for styling
   - Lucide React icons
   - Excel parsing with openpyxl/pandas (reading cell fill colors)
   - Network IP address management concepts (DHCP pool, static range, fixed IPs)
   - IP range grouping for display
   - Clipboard API for copy functionality

3. Files and Code Sections:

content.text:
Good question! To make the IP Address Manager able to write back to your Excel file, I'd need to set up a backend since React runs in the browser and can't directly write to files on your computer.

There are a few approaches we could take:

1. **Export to new Excel file** - Add a "Download as Excel" button that generates a new .xlsx file with your current data (including any changes). This works purely in the browser using a library like SheetJS.

2. **Local file sync** - Create a simple Node.js script that runs on your machine, watches for changes, and updates the Excel file.

3. **Full web app with backend** - Build a small Express/Node server that handles reading and writing to the Excel file, with the React UI as the frontend.

Which approach interests you most? For home network management, option 1 (export/download) is often the simplest - you'd make changes in the UI, then download the updated spreadsheet when you're done. Would that work for your needs, or do you need real-time sync with the original file?

I've built out the full editable IP Address Manager with all the features you requested:

**New Features:**

1. **Edit existing IPs** - Click on any card to expand it, then click "Edit" to open a modal where you can change the asset name, hostname, type, location, and services

2. **Claim free IPs** - Click "Claim This IP" on any free address to open a form where you fill in the details for your new server/container

3. **Release IPs** - When editing an assigned IP, click "Release IP" to mark it as free again

4. **Download as Excel** - Green button in the header exports your current data (including all changes) to a properly formatted .xlsx file with the same column structure as your original spreadsheet

5. **Unsaved changes indicator** - Shows an amber warning when you have pending changes that haven't been exported yet

**Workflow:**
1. Make changes in the UI (edit entries, claim free IPs, release IPs)
2. The "Unsaved changes" indicator appears
3. When done, click "Download Excel" to save your updated spreadsheet
4. Save the downloaded file to replace your original or keep as a new version

[View your updated IP Address Manager](computer:///sessions/keen-focused-galileo/mnt/outputs/ip-address-manager.jsx)

The app uses SheetJS (xlsx library) for Excel export, which is imported at the top of the file. When you run this in a React environment, you'll need to have SheetJS installed (`npm install xlsx`).

# IP Address Manager

A React app for managing your home network's static IP addresses.

## Requirements

- [Node.js](https://nodejs.org/) v16 or higher (includes npm)

## Setup & Run

```bash
# 1. Install dependencies (only needed once)
npm install

# 2. Start the app
npm run dev
```

Then open your browser to **http://localhost:5173**

## Build for Production

To create a standalone build you can host anywhere:

```bash
npm run build
```

Output will be in the `dist/` folder — just serve that with any web server.

## Features

- Search by IP, hostname, service, or location
- Edit any existing IP entry
- Claim free/available IPs with a form
- Release IPs back to the free pool
- Download as Excel (.xlsx) to save your changes
- Cards and Table views
- DHCP/Static/Fixed/Free IP classification

The git repo is initialised with your first commit. Now, on your machine, open a terminal in the `ip-manager` folder (from the zip you downloaded) and run these three commands:

```bash
# 1. Create the repo on GitHub (you'll be prompted to log in if needed)
gh repo create ip-manager --public --source=. --push

# OR if you prefer the manual way without the GitHub CLI:

# 1. Go to github.com/new and create a repo called "ip-manager"
# 2. Then run:
git remote add origin https://github.com/YOUR_USERNAME/ip-manager.git
git push -u origin main
```

The quickest route is using the **GitHub CLI** (`gh`) — if you don't have it, you can install it from [cli.github.com](https://cli.github.com). Run `gh auth login` once to authenticate, then the single `gh repo create` command does everything in one go.
